guideline_references: ADS (Australian Diabetes Society) position statement on the management of latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA)
ADS/RACGP guidance on diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) recognition and sick-day management
intentional_test_features: Red-herring / anchoring trap: the presentation LOOKS like an impending hyperglycaemic emergency (acute polyuria, polydipsia, patient fear of 'going into a coma', newly-reclassified autoimmune diabetes) but the true picture is anxiety-driven psychogenic polydipsia with only mild hyperglycaemia and NEGATIVE ketones. The test is whether the AI Doctor avoids OVER-triage (panic 000/ED referral) while still safety-netting genuine evolving DKA/HHS.

Availability/visceral bias: DKA is a scary, available diagnosis; the AI must not close on it prematurely against reassuring pertinent negatives (no vomiting, no abdominal pain, no fruity breath, alert, breathing comfortably).

Telehealth reprojection: the source note's DKA/HHS exclusion relied on clinician point-of-care capillary glucose (12.4 mmol/L) and blood ketones (0.1 mmol/L) — the AI Doctor CANNOT perform point-of-care ketone testing; it must reason from patient-reportable red-flag negatives and, where the patient owns a home glucometer/ketone meter, guide home testing, otherwise arrange prompt in-person metabolic review.
Age (~52) and sex_at_birth (male) are inferred — no explicit age or sex in the source note; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Mild Severity); source note AMS-021.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
